items.title: Players On Bad Teams Can Be Good For Fantasy Football
items.summary: Justin Edwards analyzed bottom-5 scoring offenses (pts/drive) 2021-2025. Bad offenses badly underperform expectation: 0 top-12 QBs (vs 9.4 expected), 11 top-24 RBs (vs 18.8), 6 top-24 WRs (vs 18.8). WHAT SURVIVES a bad offense: (1) volume-monopoly RBs at 70%+ snaps — David Montgomery, Jonathan Taylor, Saquon Barkley, Tony Pollard, Ashton Jeanty; (2) target-hog WRs — Garrett Wilson (30.4% share), Chris Olave (27.2%), Malik Nabers (32.2%) — production through concentration, though ceilings stay capped; (3) TEs with weak WR competition. AVOID on bad offenses: every QB (none finished top-12 in 5 yrs — explicitly flags Malik Willis); secondary WRs (Wan'Dale Robinson, Jalen Coker, Omar Cooper Jr., Adonai Mitchell); and early-down-only RBs with no receiving work (the Quinshon Judkins archetype: 50% snaps but 7% target share).

items.title: Fantasy Factors: Understanding Route Depth (FantasyPoints)
items.summary_src: grounded in the author's own tweet thread (full article is subscriber-gated)
items.summary: Zain Dhanani's counterintuitive finding from charting route depth: deep threats are NOT the fantasy boom players people assume. WRs who run DEEPER route trees are NOT higher-ceiling week to week — those with SHALLOWER trees both boom MORE and bust LESS. Practical read: for weekly floor+ceiling, favor underneath/intermediate target-earners over pure deep threats. Route-role examples he cites: Jaylen Waddle works short/intermediate, Pat Bryant intermediate, Courtland Sutton the deep role — implying Waddle's shallower profile is the more fantasy-friendly of the group. (RyanJ_Heath amplified the same takeaway.)

items.title: TEE > LADD — Tee Higgins vs Ladd McConkey (Fantasy Points)
items.summary_src: from the posting tweet + video title (not transcribed — needs the local whisper tool)
items.summary: A Fantasy Points video staking out the Tee Higgins over Ladd McConkey position at the 3/4 draft turn: "I will not draft Ladd McConkey over Tee Higgins, no matter what... that's a bridge too far." The room's read is Higgins as the firmer back-of-3rd/top-4th anchor over McConkey.

items.title: Route depth — companion podcast
items.summary_src: companion to the route-depth article (not transcribed)
items.summary: Audio companion to Dhanani's route-depth piece — same thesis: shallower route trees are the more reliable fantasy profile; deep-threat usage is over-valued for weekly scoring.

items.title: Denzel Boston — offseason profile (32 Beat Writers)
items.summary_src: from the posting tweet quote (video not transcribed)
items.summary: Profile of rookie WR Denzel Boston: per the beat writers he 'hit the ground running' this offseason and showed he's 'more than just a big-bodied target in the red zone.' Drafted after Concepcion, but may have 'stolen the show in the offseason' — an early camp-buzz signal on his role beyond red-zone work.

items.title: The 2 sleeper QBs to draft in all formats (Winks & Norris)
items.summary_src: from the posting tweets (video not transcribed — the two QBs aren't named in the posts)
items.summary: Hayden Winks and Josh Norris pitch two late-round 'perfect' sleeper QB targets to draft in all formats as the value fallback if you miss the elite tier. The specific two QBs aren't named in the tweets — identifying them needs the video transcript (local whisper tool).

items.title: Is Zero-RB dead in 2026? (Fantasy Points)
items.summary_src: from the posting tweet (video not transcribed)
items.summary: A draft-strategy debate: "Zero RB was never alive as far as I'm concerned... it didn't die, it never even had a life." The panel questions whether loading up on receivers early (Zero-RB) is still viable in 2026 — a construction-philosophy discussion rather than a player-specific take.

courtland sutton.title: Fantasy Factors: Understanding Route Depth (FantasyPoints)
courtland sutton.summary: Zain Dhanani's counterintuitive finding from charting route depth: deep threats are NOT the fantasy boom players people assume. WRs who run DEEPER route trees are NOT higher-ceiling week to week — those with SHALLOWER trees both boom MORE and bust LESS. Practical read: for weekly floor+ceiling, favor underneath/intermediate target-earners over pure deep threats. Route-role examples he cites: Jaylen Waddle works short/intermediate, Pat Bryant intermediate, Courtland Sutton the deep role — implying Waddle's shallower profile is the more fantasy-friendly of the group. (RyanJ_Heath amplified the same takeaway.)
courtland sutton.summary_src: grounded in the author's own tweet thread (full article is subscriber-gated)
